title: Cheque Dishonour in Bangladesh – Section 138 NI Act Guide
metaTitle: Cheque Dishonour Bangladesh – Section 138 NI Act Full Guide
metaDescription: Complete guide to cheque dishonour cases in Bangladesh under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act — how to file, procedure, punishment, and defences.
heroIntro: A dishonoured cheque is not just a banking inconvenience — in Bangladesh it is a criminal offence. Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act 1881 provides a powerful mechanism to hold the cheque issuer criminally liable and recover the money owed. But the procedure is technical and time-sensitive. Missing a single deadline can forfeit your right to sue.
toc: What Is Cheque Dishonour Under Bangladesh Law?
The Legal Framework: Section 138 NI Act 1881
When Is a Cheque Dishonour Case Valid?
Step-by-Step: How to File a Section 138 Case
Documents You Need to File the Case
Punishment If the Accused Is Convicted

sections.h2: What Is Cheque Dishonour Under Bangladesh Law?
sections.content:
A cheque is dishonoured when a bank returns it unpaid. Reasons include: insufficient funds, account closed, payment stopped by the drawer, signature mismatch, or post-dated cheque presented early. While dishonour for technical reasons (e.g., date format) may not attract criminal liability, dishonour for insufficiency of funds or where the amount exceeds the amount arranged triggers criminal liability under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act 1881 (NI Act).

This is one of Bangladesh's most commonly filed criminal cases — particularly in commercial disputes, loan recoveries, and business transactions where cheques are used as security. An experienced criminal lawyer in Dhaka can file and prosecute a Section 138 case efficiently.

sections.h2: The Legal Framework: Section 138 NI Act 1881
sections.content:
Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act 1881 (as applicable in Bangladesh) provides:

  • A person who draws a cheque for discharge of a debt or liability commits an offence if the cheque is dishonoured.
  • The dishonour must be due to insufficient funds or the amount exceeding the amount arranged.
  • Conviction carries punishment of imprisonment up to one year, or fine up to three times the cheque amount, or both.
  • The offence is cognisable in the Magistrate's court.

Sections 139–142 provide additional related provisions — including the presumption that a cheque was issued for discharge of a liability (reversing the burden of proof to the accused), time limits for filing, and conditions precedent.

sections.h2: When Is a Cheque Dishonour Case Valid?
sections.content:
Not every dishonoured cheque gives rise to a Section 138 criminal case. The following conditions must all be satisfied:

  • The cheque was for discharge of a legally enforceable debt or liability — not a gift, donation, or illegal transaction.
  • The bank dishonoured the cheque for insufficiency of funds (or exceeding arranged amount) — not for a purely technical reason such as a date error or signature mismatch unrelated to funds.
  • The payee sent a legally valid 30-day notice to the drawer demanding payment within 30 days of receiving the dishonour memo from the bank.
  • The drawer failed to pay within 30 days of receiving the notice.
  • The case was filed within 30 days of the notice period expiry — this is a firm deadline. Missing it bars the case.

If any condition is not met, the Magistrate will reject the complaint. Accuracy in the notice and timing is critical.

sections.h2: Step-by-Step: How to File a Section 138 Case
sections.content:
The procedure to initiate a cheque dishonour case in Bangladesh:

  1. Present the cheque to the bank and obtain the dishonour memo (return memo) specifying the reason for dishonour.
  2. Send the 30-day legal notice to the drawer within a reasonable time of receiving the dishonour memo — by registered post or courier with receipt. The notice must demand payment of the cheque amount as a condition to avoid prosecution.
  3. If the drawer fails to pay within 30 days of the notice, file a complaint (naraji petition) before the Chief Metropolitan Magistrate (CMM) in Dhaka or the Chief Judicial Magistrate (CJM) of the relevant district.
  4. The Magistrate examines the complaint and may summon the accused if the case appears prima facie valid.
  5. Trial proceeds with the accused given an opportunity to present defences. The burden shifts to the accused to disprove the presumption raised under Section 139.
  6. Conviction or acquittal.

The legal notice is the most technically critical step. It must:

  • Be sent in writing to the drawer at their last known address.
  • Demand payment of the cheque amount clearly.
  • Be sent by registered post or a method that creates a documented proof of receipt.
  • Give the drawer 30 days to make payment from receipt of the notice.
  • Contain accurate details of the cheque (number, date, amount, bank).

Any defect in the notice — wrong address, insufficient demand, or lack of proof of delivery — can be used by the defence to get the case dismissed. Your lawyer should draft and send the notice to ensure it is legally airtight. Keep the original postal receipts and the bank's dishonour memo carefully — they are the core exhibits in the trial.

sections.h2: Documents You Need to File the Case
sections.content:
Gather the following before filing:

  • Original dishonoured cheque
  • Bank's dishonour/return memo (specifying reason)
  • Copy of the 30-day legal notice sent to the drawer
  • Proof of delivery of the notice (registered post receipt, courier acknowledgement)
  • NID of the complainant
  • Any underlying agreement, contract, or document evidencing the debt
  • Bank statements showing the transaction

The original cheque is the primary exhibit. Keep it safe and present the original at every hearing — do not allow the original to be misplaced or damaged.

sections.h2: Punishment If the Accused Is Convicted
sections.content:
Upon conviction under Section 138, the Court may order:

  • Imprisonment up to 1 year
  • Fine up to three times the cheque amount
  • Both imprisonment and fine

In practice, courts frequently impose both a fine (equivalent to the cheque amount or more) and a period of imprisonment (often suspended or reduced on appeal). The accused may also be ordered to pay compensation to the complainant. The prospect of criminal conviction often pressures accused persons to settle and pay before trial — making these cases powerful recovery tools even before judgment.

If you are the drawer (accused), the following defences may be available:

  • No legally enforceable debt: The cheque was not issued for a genuine debt or liability but as a gift, blank security, or under duress.
  • Cheque was stolen or forged: You did not issue the cheque voluntarily.
  • Procedural defects: The notice was defective, not properly received, or the case was filed after the 30-day deadline.
  • Payment already made: The amount was paid before the case was filed.
  • Post-dating without liability: The cheque was post-dated expressly as a future guarantee for an obligation that never arose.

The key advantage for the complainant under Section 139 is the presumption that the cheque was issued for a debt — which means the drawer must prove otherwise. A weak defence without clear evidence rarely succeeds. Contact a criminal defence lawyer immediately if you are served with a Section 138 notice.

sections.h2: Real-Life Scenario: Business Partner Dishonours a Cheque
sections.content:
Karim lent Tk. 10 lakh to a business partner who gave him a post-dated cheque as repayment. On the agreed date, Karim presented the cheque and the bank returned it marked "insufficient funds."

Karim's lawyer took the following steps:

  1. Obtained the bank's dishonour memo and preserved the original cheque.
  2. Sent a registered legal notice the next day, demanding payment within 30 days.
  3. The drawer paid nothing by the 30th day.
  4. Filed a Section 138 complaint before the CMM Dhaka within 25 days of the notice expiry.
  5. At the first hearing, the Magistrate found the complaint prima facie valid and issued a summons.

Faced with a criminal summons and the prospect of conviction, the drawer contacted Karim's lawyer and agreed to a full settlement — paying the Tk. 10 lakh plus compensation. The case was withdrawn by mutual consent.

faqs.question: Is cheque dishonour a criminal offence in Bangladesh?
faqs.answer: Yes. Under Section 138 of the Negotiable Instruments Act 1881, cheque dishonour due to insufficient funds is a criminal offence punishable by up to one year imprisonment or a fine of up to three times the cheque amount, or both.

faqs.question: What is the time limit to file a cheque dishonour case?
faqs.answer: The complainant must send a 30-day legal notice after the dishonour, then file the case within 30 days of the notice period expiring. Missing this deadline bars the criminal case.

faqs.question: Do I need a lawyer to file a Section 138 case?
faqs.answer: While technically anyone can file, it is strongly advisable to use a lawyer. The notice must be legally precise and the case filed within strict time limits — any error can get the case dismissed at the threshold.

faqs.question: Can I file both a criminal case and a civil money recovery suit for the same cheque?
faqs.answer: Yes. A Section 138 criminal case and a civil suit for recovery of the cheque amount are independent remedies. Both can be pursued simultaneously.

faqs.question: What if the drawer pays after the case is filed?
faqs.answer: If the drawer pays the full amount owed, the complainant can withdraw the criminal case. Courts are generally amenable to withdrawal upon proof of full payment and settlement.
